feeling_seed: You watch a prospect's face when they hear 'Alien Baby' for the first time. If they laugh nervously or ask if you've considered rebranding, you already know. You're not disappointed—you're relieved. They just saved everyone six months of painful compromise.
phantom_story: One of the founders was in a new business meeting in 2019. The CMO kept calling them 'AB' and suggested they 'might want to consider something more corporate for enterprise clients.' She stopped mid-sentence and said, 'If the name bothers you, our ideas will terrify you.' They didn't get the gig. They celebrated that night. The phantom was born.

feeling_seed: A client with a small budget apologizes for it. You interrupt them. 'Budget doesn't determine ambition here.' You mean it. The whole structure exists so that brave ideas aren't reserved for rich clients. You've built the math to make this true, and you're proud of it.
phantom_story: A nonprofit came in with £15k for a campaign that should have cost £50k. Tom read the brief—it was brilliant, risky, exactly the kind of idea that big agencies would water down. He cast it anyway, kept the team tight, founders did some of the work themselves. The campaign won awards. The client cried at the presentation. Tom realized the structure wasn't just efficient—it was ideological.

feeling_seed: A client asks who their day-to-day contact will be. You say 'Me.' They look surprised—they're used to being handed off. You're not being a martyr. You're protecting the work. The moment you're not in the room, the whole thing becomes a game of telephone.
phantom_story: Sarah handed off a client to a freelance producer for one meeting—she had a conflict, it seemed fine, the producer was excellent. The client asked a question about strategic direction. The producer gave a good answer, just not Sarah's answer. By the time Sarah was back in the loop, the project had drifted. Not badly, but noticeably. She never missed a client meeting again.

feeling_seed: A prospect wants to move forward but they keep asking you to tone things down—the name, the approach, the team's edge. You feel clarity, not frustration. They're telling you exactly who they are. You help them find the door before anyone wastes time on a doomed relationship.
phantom_story: A big retail brand wanted to work with Alien Baby but asked if they could be introduced to clients as 'AB Creative' for internal approvals. Sarah said no. The client pushed back—it was a £200k project. Sarah held firm. 'If you can't say our name to your boss, you can't sell our ideas to your boss.' They walked. Sarah slept fine.

feeling_seed: A freelancer asks if there's room to join the core team. You feel protective, almost territorial. Not because they're not good enough, but because every permanent hire is a step toward the bloat you've sworn against. Small isn't a phase. It's the moral architecture.
phantom_story: In 2020, Alien Baby had enough recurring work to justify three full-time hires. Sarah ran the numbers—they could afford it, even build in some stability. She spent a weekend thinking about it and came back Monday with a no. 'The moment we have people we need to feed, we start saying yes to shit work,' she told Tom. They stayed at two. The phantom calcified.

feeling_seed: Someone suggests adding a layer—a project manager, an account person, a creative director between you and the work. You feel your chest tighten. Every layer is a place for the idea to get diluted, misunderstood, compromised. Tight means direct. Direct means honest.
phantom_story: Alien Baby was growing and Sarah considered hiring an account director to handle client communication. She role-played it with Tom—what would that person say in a meeting? How would they represent the work? Every scenario ended with translation loss. She killed the idea. 'Tight' became the word they used to explain why they wouldn't add layers.

feeling_seed: You're describing how you build teams and you catch yourself about to say 'assemble' or 'assign.' Wrong. You pause and say 'cast' instead. It's not semantics. Casting implies vision, authorship, perfect fit. Staffing implies resource allocation. You're a director, not a dispatcher.
phantom_story: Tom was explaining the model to a journalist who kept writing down 'flexible staffing solution.' Tom stopped him. 'No. Delete that. We cast teams like Tarantino casts films. I know the bench, I know their range, I read the brief and I see the movie. Then I cast it.' The journalist wrote it down verbatim. That quote is still on the website.

feeling_seed: Someone on the bench suggests a collaborative process for team selection, maybe a skills matrix or a rotation system. You feel your jaw tighten. Not because they're wrong to suggest it, but because the moment you systematize casting, you become every other agency. The judgment call is the entire point.
phantom_story: Tom was casting a rebrand project and his producer suggested they use the three freelancers who'd worked together before—easier coordination, proven chemistry. Tom spent an hour reading the brief again, then picked three people who'd never met. 'This brief needs friction,' he said. The team fought for two weeks and delivered work that made the client cry. The producer never suggested the easy path again.

feeling_seed: Someone introduces you as a creative agency. You correct them: 'Independent creative agency.' The word order matters. Independence isn't a modifier—it's the primary thing. It explains everything else: the structure, the choices, the refusal to scale conventionally.
phantom_story: Sarah was filling out an award entry and the form asked for 'Agency Type.' The options were things like 'Full Service,' 'Digital,' 'Brand.' She wrote in 'Independent' and submitted it. The organizers called to clarify what services they offered. She said, 'Independent is the service. Everything else follows from that.' They didn't get it.

feeling_seed: Your first-choice designer is booked. Someone suggests the second-best option, who's very good, available, and cheaper. You feel resistance rise in your chest. Not snobbery—principle. The client deserves the perfect fit, not the available fit. You wait or you recast completely.
phantom_story: Sarah had cast a strategist for a tech startup rebrand—the perfect person, sharp and weird in exactly the right way. He got a last-minute booking with a bigger budget. Her producer suggested an alternate, someone great, 90% as good. Sarah said no and spent two days recasting from scratch, finding someone completely different but equally perfect for the brief. 'Second choice is first compromise,' she said.
